text: Love justice, you that are the judges of the earth. Think of the Lord in goodness, and seek him in simplicity of heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he is found by them that tempt him not: and he sheweth himself to them that have faith in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For perverse thoughts separate from God: and his power, when it is tried, reproveth the unwise:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For wisdom will not enter into a malicious soul, nor dwell in a body subject to sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the Holy Spirit of discipline will flee from the deceitful, and will withdraw himself from thoughts that are without understanding, and he shall not abide when iniquity cometh in.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the spirit of wisdom is benevolent, and will not acquit the evil speaker from his lips: for God is witness of his reins, and he is a true searcher of his heart, and a hearer of his tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the Spirit of the Lord hath filled the whole world: and that which containeth all things, hath knowledge of the voice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore he that speaketh unjust things, cannot be hid, neither shall the chastising judgment pass him by.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For inquisition shall be made into the thoughts of the ungodly, and the hearing of his words shall come to God, to the chastising of his iniquities.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the ear of jealousy heareth all things, and the tumult of murmuring shall not be hid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep yourselves, therefore, from murmuring, which profiteth nothing, and refrain your tongue from detraction, for an obscure speech shall not go for nought: and the mouth that belieth, killeth the soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Seek not death in the error of your life, neither procure ye destruction by the works of your hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God made not death, neither hath he pleasure in the destruction of the living.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he created all things that they might be: and he made the nations of the earth for health: and there is no poison of destruction in them, nor kingdom of hell upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For justice is perpetual and immortal.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the wicked with works and words have called it to them: and esteeming it a friend, have fallen away and have made a covenant with it: because they are worthy to be of the part thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they have said, reasoning with themselves, but not right: The time of our life is short and tedious, and in the end of a man there is no remedy, and no man hath been known to have returned from hell:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For we are born of nothing, and after this we shall be as if we had not been: for the breath in our nostrils is smoke: and speech a spark to move our heart,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which being put out, our body shall be ashes, and our spirit shall be poured abroad as soft air, and our life shall pass away as the trace of a cloud, and shall be dispersed as a mist, which is driven away by the beams of the sun, and overpowered with the heat thereof:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And our name in time shall be forgotten, and no man shall have any remembrance of our works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For our time is as the passing of a shadow, and there is no going back of our end: for it is fast sealed, and no man returneth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come, therefore, and let us enjoy the good things that are present, and let us speedily use the creatures as in youth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us fill ourselves with costly wine, and ointments: and let not the flower of the time pass by us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us crown ourselves with roses, before they be withered: let no meadow escape our riot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let none of us go without his part in luxury: let us every where leave tokens of joy: for this is our portion, and this our lot.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us oppress the poor just man, and not spare the widow, nor honour the ancient grey hairs of the aged.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But let our strength be the law of justice: for that which is feeble is found to be nothing worth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us, therefore, lie in wait for the just, because he is not for our turn, and he is contrary to our doings, and upbraideth us with transgressions of the law, and divulgeth against us the sins of our way of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He boasteth that he hath the knowledge of God, and calleth himself the son of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He is become a censurer of our thoughts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He is grievous unto us, even to behold: for his life is not like other men’s, and his ways are very different.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We are esteemed by him as triflers, and he abstaineth from our ways as from filthiness, and he preferreth the latter end of the just, and glorieth that he hath God for his father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us see then if his words be true, and let us prove what shall happen to him, and we shall know what his end shall be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if he be the true son of God, he will defend him, and will deliver him from the hands of his enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us examine him by outrages and tortures, that we may know his meekness, and try his patience.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us condemn him to a most shameful death: for there shall be respect had unto him by his words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These things they thought, and were deceived: for their own malice blinded them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they knew not the secrets of God, nor hoped for the wages of justice, nor esteemed the honour of holy souls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God created man incorruptible, and to the image of his own likeness he made him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But by the envy of the devil, death came into the world:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they follow him that are of his side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the souls of the just are in the hand of God, and the torment of death shall not touch them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the sight of the unwise they seemed to die: and their departure was taken for misery:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And their going away from us, for utter destruction: but they are in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And though in the sight of men they suffered torments, their hope is full of immortality.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Afflicted in few things, in many they shall be well rewarded: because God hath tried them, and found them worthy of himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As gold in the furnace, he hath proved them, and as a victim of a holocaust, he hath received them, and in time there shall be respect had to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The just shall shine, and shall run to and fro like sparks among the reeds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall judge nations, and rule over people, and their Lord shall reign for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that trust in him shall understand the truth: and they that are faithful in love, shall rest in him: for grace and peace are to his elect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the wicked shall be punished according to their own devices: who have neglected the just, and have revolted from the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he that rejecteth wisdom, and discipline, is unhappy: and their hope is vain, and their labours without fruit, and their works unprofitable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their wives are foolish, and their children wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their offspring is cursed, for happy is the barren: and the undefiled, that hath not known bed in sin, she shall have fruit in the visitation of holy souls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the eunuch, that hath not wrought iniquity with his hands, nor thought wicked things against God for the precious gift of faith shall be given to him, and a most acceptable lot in the temple of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the fruit of good labours is glorious, and the root of wisdom never faileth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the children of adulterers shall not come to perfection, and the seed of the unlawful bed shall be rooted out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if they live long, they shall be nothing regarded, and their last old age shall be without honour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if they die quickly, they shall have no hope, nor speech of comfort in the day of trial.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For dreadful are the ends of a wicked race.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How beautiful is the chaste generation with glory: for the memory thereof is immortal: because it is known both with God and with men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When it is present, they imitate it: and they desire it, when it hath withdrawn itself, and it triumpheth crowned for ever, winning the reward of undefiled conflicts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the multiplied brood of the wicked shall not thrive, and bastard slips shall not take deep root, nor any fast foundation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if they flourish in branches for a time, yet standing not fast, they shall be shaken with the wind, and through the force of winds they shall be rooted out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the branches not being perfect, shall be broken, and their fruits shall be unprofitable, and sour to eat, and fit for nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the children that are born of unlawful beds, are witnesses of wickedness against their parents in their trial.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the just man, if he be prevented with death, shall be in rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For venerable old age is not that of long time, nor counted by the number of years: but the understanding of a man is grey hairs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a spotless life is old age.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He pleased God, and was beloved, and living among sinners, he was translated.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He was taken away, lest wickedness should alter his understanding, or deceit beguile his soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the bewitching of vanity obscureth good things, and the wandering of concupiscence overturneth the innocent mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Being made perfect in a short space, he fulfilled a long time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For his soul pleased God: therefore he hastened to bring him out of the midst of iniquities: but the people see this, and understand not, nor lay up such things in their hearts:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That the grace of God, and his mercy is with his saints, and that he hath respect to his chosen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the just that is dead, condemneth the wicked that are living, and youth soon ended, the long life of the unjust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they shall see the end of the wise man, and it shall not understand what God hath designed for him, and why the Lord hath set him in safety.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall see him, and shall despise him: but the Lord shall laugh them to scorn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall fall after this without honour, and be a reproach among the dead for ever: for he shall burst them puffed up and speechless, and shall shake them from the foundations, and they shall be utterly laid waste: they shall be in sorrow, and their memory shall perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall come with fear at the thought of their sins, and their iniquities shall stand against them to convict them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shall the just stand with great constancy against those that have afflicted them, and taken away their labours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These seeing it, shall be troubled with terrible fear, and shall be amazed at the suddenness of their unexpected salvation,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying within themselves, repenting, and groaning for anguish of spirit: These are they, whom we had sometime in derision, and for a parable of reproach.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We fools esteemed their life madness, and their end without honour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold, how they are numbered among the children of God, and their lot is among the saints.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore we have erred from the way of truth, and the light of justice hath not shined unto us, and the sun of understanding hath not risen upon us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We wearied ourselves in the way of iniquity and destruction, and have walked through hard ways, but the way of the Lord we have not known.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What hath pride profited us? or what advantage hath the boasting of riches brought us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All those things are passed away like a shadow, and like a post that runneth on,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as a ship that passeth through the waves: whereof when it is gone by, the trace cannot be found, nor the path of its keel in the waters:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or as when a bird flieth through the air, of the passage of which no mark can be found, but only the sound of the wings beating the light air, and parting it by the force of her flight: she moved her wings, and hath flown through, and there is no mark found afterwards of her way:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or as when an arrow is shot at a mark, the divided air quickly cometh together again, so that the passage thereof is not known:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So we also being born, forthwith ceased to be: and have been able to shew no mark of virtue: but are consumed in our wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Such things as these the sinners said in hell:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the hope of the wicked is as dust, which is blown away with the wind, and as a thin froth which is dispersed by the storm: and a smoke that is scattered abroad by the wind: and as the remembrance of a guest of one day that passeth by.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the just shall live for evermore: and their reward is with the Lord, and the care of them with the most High.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore shall they receive a kingdom of glory, and a crown of beauty at the hand of the Lord: for with his right hand he will cover them, and with his holy arm he will defend them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his zeal will take armour, and he will arm the creature for the revenge of his enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will put on justice as a breastplate, and will take true judgment instead of a helmet:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He will take equity for an invincible shield:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he will sharpen his severe wrath for a spear, and the whole world shall fight with him against the unwise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then shafts of lightning shall go directly from the clouds, as from a bow well bent, they shall be shot out, and shall fly to the mark.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thick hail shall be cast upon them from the stone casting wrath: the water of the sea shall rage against them, and the rivers shall run together in a terrible manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A mighty wind shall stand up against them, and as a whirlwind shall divide them: and their iniquity shall bring all the earth to a desert, and wickedness shall overthrow the thrones of the mighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom is better than strength: and a wise man is better than a strong man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, therefore, ye kings, and understand, learn ye that are judges of the ends of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give ear, you that rule the people, and that please yourselves in multitudes of nations:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For power is given you by the Lord, and strength by the most High, who will examine your works: and search out your thoughts:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because being ministers of his kingdom, you have not judged rightly, nor kept the law of justice, nor walked according to the will of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Horribly and speedily will he appear to you: for a most severe judgment shall be for them that bear rule.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For to him that is little, mercy is granted: but the mighty shall be mightily tormented.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God will not except any man’s person, neither will he stand in awe of any man’s greatness: for he made the little and the great, and he hath equally care of all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But a greater punishment is ready for the more mighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To you, therefore, O kings, are these my words, that you may learn wisdom, and not fall from it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they that have kept just things justly, shall be justified: and they that have learned these things, shall find what to answer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Covet ye, therefore, my words, and love them, and you shall have instruction.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom is glorious, and never fadeth away, and is easily seen by them that love her, and is found by them that seek her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She preventeth them that covet her, so that she first sheweth herself unto them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that awaketh early to seek her, shall not labour: for he shall find her sitting at his door.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To think, therefore, upon her, is perfect understanding: and he that watcheth for her, shall quickly be secure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she goeth about seeking such as are worthy of her, and she sheweth herself to them cheerfully in the ways, and meeteth them with all providence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the beginning of her is the most true desire of discipline.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the care of discipline is love: and love is the keeping of her laws: and the keeping of her laws is the firm foundation of incorruption:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And incorruption bringeth near to God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore the desire of wisdom bringeth to the everlasting kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If then your delight be in thrones, and sceptres, O ye kings of the people, love wisdom, that you may reign for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Love the light of wisdom, all ye that bear rule over peoples.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now what wisdom is, and what was her origin, I will declare: and I will not hide from you the mysteries of God, but will seek her out from the beginning of her birth, and bring the knowledge of her to light, and will not pass over the truth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither will I go with consuming envy: for such a man shall not be partaker of wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the multitude of the wise is the welfare of the whole world: and a wise king is the upholding of the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Receive, therefore, instruction by my words, and it shall be profitable to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I myself also am a mortal man, like all others, and of the race of him, that was first made of the earth, and in the womb of my mother I was fashioned to be flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the time of ten months I was compacted in blood, of the seed of man, and the pleasure of sleep concurring.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being born, I drew in the common air, and fell upon the earth, that is made alike, and the first voice which I uttered was crying, as all others do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I was nursed in swaddling clothes, and with great cares.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For none of the kings had any other beginning of birth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all men have one entrance into life, and the like going out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore I wished, and understanding was given me: and I called upon God, and the spirit of wisdom came upon me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I preferred her before kingdoms and thrones, and esteemed riches nothing in comparison of her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither did I compare unto her any precious stone: for all gold, in comparison of her, is as a little sand; and silver, in respect to her, shall be counted as clay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I loved her above health and beauty, and chose to have her instead of light: for her light cannot be put out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now all good things came to me together with her, and innumerable riches through her hands,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I rejoiced in all these: for this wisdom went before me, and I knew not that she was the mother of them all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which I have learned without guile, and communicate without envy, and her riches I hide not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she is an infinite treasure to men: which they that use, become the friends of God, being commended for the gifts of discipline.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God hath given to me to speak as I would, and to conceive thoughts worthy of those things that are given me: because he is the guide of wisdom, and the director of the wise:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in his hand are both we, and our words, and all wisdom, and the knowledge and skill of works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he hath given me the true knowledge of the things that are: to know the disposition of the whole world, and the virtues of the elements,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beginning, and ending, and midst of the times, the alterations of their courses, and the changes of seasons,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The revolutions of the year, and the dispositions of the stars,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The natures of living creatures, and rage of wild beasts, the force of winds, and reasonings of men, the diversities of plants, and the virtues of roots,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all such things as are hid, and not foreseen, I have learned: for wisdom, which is the worker of all things, taught me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in her is the spirit of understanding; holy, one, manifold, subtile, eloquent, active, undefiled, sure, sweet, loving that which is good, quick, which nothing hindereth, beneficent,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gentle, kind, steadfast, assured, secure, having all power, overseeing all things, and containing all spirits: intelligible, pure, subtile:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For wisdom is more active than all active things; and reacheth everywhere, by reason of her purity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she is a vapour of the power of God, and a certain pure emmanation of the glory of the Almighty God: and therefore no defiled thing cometh into her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she is the brightness of eternal light, and the unspotted mirror of God’s majesty, and the image of his goodness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being but one, she can do all things: and remaining in herself the same, she reneweth all things, and through nations conveyeth herself into holy souls, she maketh the friends of God and prophets.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For God loveth none but him that dwelleth with wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she is more beautiful than the sun, and above all the order of the stars: being compared with the light, she is found before it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For after this cometh night, but no evil can overcome wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She reacheth, therefore, from end to end mightily, and ordereth all things sweetly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Her have I loved, and have sought her out from my youth, and have desired to take for my spouse, and I became a lover of her beauty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She glorifieth her nobility by being conversant with God: yea, and the Lord of all things hath loved her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is she that teacheth the knowledge of God and is the chooser of his works.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if riches be desired in life, what is richer than wisdom, which maketh all things?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if sense do work: who is a more artful worker than she of those things that are?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if a man love justice: her labours have great virtues: for she teacheth temperance, and prudence, and justice, and fortitude, which are such things as men can have nothing more profitable in life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if a man desire much knowledge: she knoweth things past, and judgeth of things to come: she knoweth the subtilties of speeches, and the solutions of arguments: she knoweth signs and wonders before they be done, and the events of times and ages.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I purposed, therefore, to take her to me to live with me: knowing that she will communicate to me of her good things, and will be a comfort in my cares and grief.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For her sake I shall have glory among the multitude, and honour with the ancients, though I be young:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I shall be found of a quick conceit in judgment, and shall be admired in the sight of the mighty, and the faces of princes shall wonder at me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall wait for me when I hold my peace, and they shall look upon me when I speak; and if I talk much, they shall lay their hands on their mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, by the means of her I shall have immortality: and shall leave behind me an everlasting memory to them that come after me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I shall set the people in order: and nations shall be subject to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Terrible kings hearing, shall be afraid of me: among the multitude I shall be found good, and valiant in war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When I go into my house, I shall repose myself with her: for her conversation hath no bitterness, nor her company any tediousness, but joy and gladness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thinking these things with myself, and pondering them in my heart, that to be allied to wisdom is immortality,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that there is great delight in her friendship, and inexhaustible riches in the works of her hands, and in the exercise of conference with her, wisdom, and glory in the communication of her words: I went about seeking, that I might take her to myself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I was a witty child, and had received a good soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas I was more good, I came to a body undefiled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as I knew that I could not otherwise be continent, except God gave it, and this also was a point of wisdom, to know whose gift it was, I went to the Lord, and besought him, and said with my whole heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God of my fathers, and Lord of mercy, who hast made all things with thy word,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And by thy wisdom hast appointed man, that he should have dominion over the creature that was made by thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That he should order the world according to equity and justice, and execute justice with an upright heart:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give me wisdom, that sitteth by thy throne, and cast me not off from among thy children:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I am thy servant, and the son of thy handmaid, a weak man, and of short time, and falling short of the understanding of judgment and laws.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if one be perfect among the children of men, yet if thy wisdom be not with him, he shall be nothing regarded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast chosen me to be king of thy people, and a judge of thy sons and daughters:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And hast commanded me to build a temple on thy holy mount, and an altar in the city of thy dwelling place, a resemblance of thy holy tabernacle, which thou hast prepared from the beginning:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thy wisdom with thee, which knoweth thy works, which then also was present when thou madest the world, and knew what was agreeable to thy eyes, and what was right in thy commandments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Send her out of thy holy heaven, and from the throne of thy majesty, that she may be with me, and may labour with me, that I may know what is acceptable with thee:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For she knoweth and understandeth all things, and shall lead me soberly in my works, and shall preserve me by her power.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So shall my works be acceptable, and I shall govern thy people justly, and shall be worthy of the throne of my father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For who among men is he that can know the counsel of God? or who can think what the will of God is?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the thoughts of mortal men are fearful, and our counsels uncertain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the corruptible body is a load upon the soul, and the earthly habitation presseth down the mind that museth upon many things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And hardly do we guess aright at things that are upon earth: and with labour do we find the things that are before us. But the things that are in heaven, who shall search out?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And who shall know thy thought, except thou give wisdom, and send thy holy Spirit from above:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so the ways of them that are upon earth may be corrected, and men may learn the things that please thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by wisdom they were healed, whosoever have pleased thee, O Lord, from the beginning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She preserved him, that was first formed by God, the father of the world, when he was created alone,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she brought him out of his sin, and gave him power to govern all things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when the unjust went away from her in his anger, he perished by the fury wherewith he murdered his brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whose cause, when water destroyed the earth, wisdom healed it again, directing the course of the just by contemptible wood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, when the nations had conspired together to consent to wickedness, she knew the just, and preserved him without blame to God, and kept him strong against the compassion for his son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She delivered the just man, who fled from the wicked that were perishing, when the fire came down upon Pentapolis:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whose land, for a testimony of their wickedness, is desolate, and smoketh to this day, and the trees bear fruits that ripen not, and a standing pillar of salt is a monument of an incredulous soul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For regarding not wisdom, they did not only slip in this, that they were ignorant of good things; but they left also unto men a memorial of their folly, so that in the things in which they sinned, they could not so much as lie hid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But wisdom hath delivered from sorrow them that attend upon her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She conducted the just, when he fled from his brother’s wrath, through the right ways, and shewed him the kingdom of God, and gave him the knowledge of the holy things, made him honourable in his labours, and accomplished his labours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the deceit of them that overreached him, she stood by him, and made him honourable.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She kept him safe from his enemies, and she defended him from seducers, and gave him a strong conflict, that he might overcome, and know that wisdom is mightier than all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She forsook not the just when he was sold, but delivered him from sinners: she went down with him into the pit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in bands she left him not, till she brought him the sceptre of the kingdom, and power against those that oppressed him: and shewed them to be liars that had accused him, and gave him everlasting glory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She delivered the just people, and blameless seed, from the nations that oppressed them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She entered into the soul of the servant of God and stood against dreadful kings in wonders and signs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she rendered to the just the wages of their labours, and conducted them in a wonderful way: and she was to them for a covert by day, and for the light of stars by night:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she brought them through the Red Sea, and carried them over through a great water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But their enemies she drowned in the sea, and from the depth of hell she brought them out. Therefore the just took the spoils of the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sung to thy holy name, O Lord, and they praised with one accord thy victorious hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For wisdom opened the mouth of the dumb, and made the tongues of infants eloquent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She prospered their works in the hands of the holy prophet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They went through wildernesses that were not inhabited, and in desert places they pitched their tents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They stood against their enemies, and revenged themselves of their adversaries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They were thirsty, and they called upon thee, and water was given them out of the high rock, and a refreshment of their thirst out of the hard stone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by what things their enemies were punished, when their drink failed them, while the children of Israel abounded therewith, and rejoiced:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By the same things they in their need were benefited.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For instead of a fountain of an ever running river, thou gavest human blood to the unjust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whilst they were diminished for a manifest reproof of their murdering the infants, thou gavest to thine abundant water unlooked for:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shewing by the thirst that was then, how thou didst exalt thine, and didst kill their adversaries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when they were tried, and chastised with mercy, they knew how the wicked were judged with wrath, and tormented.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou didst admonish and try them as a father: but the others, as a severe king, thou didst examine and condemn.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whether absent or present, they were tormented alike.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a double affliction came upon them, and a groaning for the remembrance of things past.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when they heard that by their punishments the others were benefited, they remembered the Lord, wondering at the end of what was come to pass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whom they scorned before, when he was thrown out at the time of his being wickedly exposed to perish, him they admired in the end, when they saw the event: their thirsting being unlike to that of the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But for the foolish devices of their iniquity, because some being deceived worshipped dumb serpents and worthless beasts, thou didst send upon them a multitude of dumb beasts for vengeance:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That they might know that by what things a man sinneth, by the same also he is tormented.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy almighty hand, which made the world of matter without form, was not unable to send upon them a multitude of bears, or fierce lions,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or unknown beasts of a new kind, full of rage; either breathing out a fiery vapour, or sending forth a stinking smoke, or shooting horrible sparks out of their eyes:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whereof not only the hurt might be able to destroy them, but also the very sight might kill them through fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea, and without these, they might have been slain with one blast, persecuted by their own deeds, and scattered by the breath of thy power: but thou hast ordered all things in measure, and number, and weight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For great power always belonged to thee alone: and who shall resist the strength of thy arm?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the whole world before thee is as the least grain of the balance, and as a drop of the morning dew, that falleth down upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou hast mercy upon all, because thou canst do all things, and overlookest the sins of men for the sake of repentance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou lovest all things that are, and hatest none of the things which thou hast made: for thou didst not appoint, or make any thing hating it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And how could any thing endure, if thou wouldst not? or be preserved, if not called by thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou sparest all: because they are thine, O Lord, who lovest souls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O how good and sweet is thy Spirit, O Lord, in all things!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And therefore thou chastisest them that err, by little and little: and admonishest them, and speakest to them, concerning the things wherein they offend: that leaving their wickedness, they may believe in thee, O Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For those ancient inhabitants of thy holy land, whom thou didst abhor,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because they did works hateful to thee by their sorceries, and wicked sacrifices,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And those merciless murderers of their own children, and eaters of men’s bowels, and devourers of blood from the midst of thy consecration,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And those parents sacrificing with their own hands helpless souls, it was thy will to destroy by the hands of our parents,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That the land which of all is most dear to thee, might receive a worthy colony of the children of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yet even those thou sparedst as men, and didst send wasps forerunners of thy host, to destroy them by little and little.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Not that thou wast unable to bring the wicked under the just by war, or by cruel beasts, or with one rough word to destroy them at once:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But executing thy judgments by degrees, thou gavest them place of repentance, not being ignorant that they were a wicked generation, and their malice natural, and that their thought could never be changed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it was a cursed seed from the beginning: neither didst thou for fear of any one give pardon to their sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For who shall say to thee: What hast thou done? or who shall withstand thy judgment? or who shall come before thee to be a revenger of wicked men? or who shall accuse thee, if the nations perish, which thou hast made?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there is no other God but thou, who hast care of all, that thou shouldst shew that thou dost not give judgment unjustly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither shall king, nor tyrant, in thy sight inquire about them whom thou hast destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For so much then, as thou art just, thou orderest all things justly: thinking it not agreeable to the power, to condemn him who deserveth not to be punished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy power is the beginning of justice: and because thou art Lord of all, thou makest thyself gracious to all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou shewest thy power, when men will not believe thee to be absolute in power, and thou convincest the boldness of them that know thee not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou being master of power, judgest with tranquillity, and with great favour disposest of us: for thy power is at hand when thou wilt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou hast taught thy people by such works, that they must be just and humane, and hast made thy children to be of a good hope: because in judging, thou givest place for repentance for sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy servants, and that deserved to die, with so great deliberation, giving them time and place whereby they might be changed from their wickedness:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With what circumspection hast thou judged thy own children, to whose parents thou hast sworn, and made covenants of good promises?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore whereas thou chastisest us, thou scourgest our enemies very many ways, to the end that when we judge we may think on thy goodness: and when we are judged, we may hope for thy mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore thou hast also greatly tormented them, who, in their life, have lived foolishly and unjustly, by the same things which they worshipped.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they went astray for a long time in the ways of error, holding those things for gods which are the most worthless among beasts, living after the manner of children without understanding.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore thou hast sent a judgment upon them, as senseless children, to mock them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that were not amended by mockeries and reprehensions, experienced the worthy judgment of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For seeing, with indignation, that they suffered by those very things which they took for gods, when they were destroyed by the same, they acknowledged him the true God, whom in time past they denied that they knew: for which cause the end also of their condemnation came upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But all men are vain, in whom there is not the knowledge of God: and who by these good things that are seen, could not understand him that is, neither by attending to the works have acknowledged who was the workman:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But have imagined either the fire, or the wind, or the swift air, or the circle of the stars, or the great water, or the sun and moon, to be the gods that rule the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With whose beauty, if they, being delighted, took them to be gods: let them know how much the Lord of them is more beautiful than they: for the first author of beauty made all those things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or if they admired their power, and their effects, let them understand by them, that he that made them, is mightier than they:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by the greatness of the beauty, and of the creature, the creator of them may be seen, so as to be known thereby.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But yet as to these they are less to be blamed. For they perhaps err, seeking God, and desirous to find him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For being conversant among his works, they search: and they are persuaded that the things are good which are seen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But then again they are not to be pardoned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if they were able to know so much as to make a judgment of the world: how did they not more easily find out the Lord thereof?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But unhappy are they, and their hope is among the dead, who have called gods the works of the hand of men, gold and silver, the inventions of art, and the resemblances of beasts, or an unprofitable stone the work of an ancient hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or if an artist, a carpenter, hath cut down a tree proper for his use in the wood, and skilfully taken off all the bark thereof, and with his art, diligently formeth a vessel profitable for the common uses of life,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And useth the chips of his work to dress his meat:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And taking what was left thereof, which is good for nothing, being a crooked piece of wood, and full of knots, carveth it diligently when he hath nothing else to do, and by the skill of his art fashioneth it, and maketh it like the image of a man:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or the resemblance of some beast, laying it over with vermilion, and painting it red, and covering every spot that is in it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And maketh a convenient dwelling place for it, and setting it in a wall, and fastening it with iron,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Providing for it, lest it should fall, knowing that it is unable to help itself: for it is an image, and hath need of help.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And then maketh prayer to it, enquiring concerning his substance, and his children, or his marriage. And he is not ashamed to speak to that which hath no life:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for health he maketh supplication to the weak, and for life prayeth to that which is dead, and for help calleth upon that which is unprofitable:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for a good journey he petitioneth him that cannot walk: and for getting, and for working, and for the event of all things he asketh him that is unable to do any thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again, another designing to sail, and beginning to make his voyage through the raging waves, calleth upon a piece of wood more frail than the wood that carrieth him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For this the desire of gain devised, and the workman built it by his skill.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thy providence, O Father, governeth it: for thou hast made a way even in the sea, and a most sure path among the waves,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shewing that thou art able to save out of all things, yea, though a man went to sea without art.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But that the works of thy wisdom might not be idle: therefore men also trust their lives even to a little wood, and passing over the sea by ship, are saved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And from the beginning also, when the proud giants perished, the hope of the world fleeing to a vessel, which was governed by thy hand, left to the world seed of generation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For blessed is the wood, by which justice cometh

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the idol that is made by hands, is cursed, as well it, as he that made it: he because he made it; and it because being frail it is called a god.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But to God the wicked and his wickedness are hateful alike.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For that which is made, together with him that made it, shall suffer torments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore there shall be no respect had even to the idols of the Gentiles: because the creatures of God are turned to an abomination, and a temptation to the souls of men, and a snare to the feet of the unwise.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the beginning of fornication is the devising of idols: and the invention of them is the corruption of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For by the vanity of men they came into the world: and therefore they shall be found to come shortly to an end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a father being afflicted with bitter grief, made to himself the image of his son, who was quickly taken away: and him who then had died as a man, he began now to worship as a god, and appointed him rites and sacrifices among his servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then, in process of time, wicked custom prevailing, this error was kept as a law, and statues were worshipped by the commandment of tyrants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And those whom men could not honour in presence, because they dwelt far off, they brought their resemblance from afar, and made an express image of the king, whom they had a mind to honour: that by this their diligence, they might honour as present, him that was absent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to the worshipping of these, the singular diligence also of the artificer helped to set forward the ignorant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he being willing to please him that employed him, laboured with all his art to make the resemblance in the best manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the multitude of men, carried away by the beauty of the work, took him now for a god, that little before was but honoured as a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this was the occasion of deceiving human life: for men serving either their affection, or their kings, gave the incommunicable name to stones and wood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was not enough for them to err about the knowledge of God, but whereas they lived in a great war of ignorance, they call so many and so great evils peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For either they sacrifice their own children, or use hidden sacrifices, or keep watches full of madness,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So that now they neither keep life, nor marriage undefiled, but one killeth another through envy, or grieveth him by adultery:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all things are mingled together, blood, murder, theft, and dissimulation, corruption and unfaithfulness, tumults and perjury, disquieting of the good,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forgetfulness of God, defiling of souls, changing of nature, disorder in marriage, and the irregularity of adultery and uncleanness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the worship of abominable idols is the cause, and the beginning and end of all evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For either they are mad when they are merry: or they prophesy lies, or they live unjustly, or easily forswear themselves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whilst they trust in idols, which are without life, though they swear amiss, they look not to be hurt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But for both these things they shall be justly punished, because they have thought not well of God, giving heed to idols, and have sworn unjustly, in guile despising justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is not the power of them, by whom they swear, but the just vengeance of sinners always punisheth the transgression of the unjust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou, our God, art gracious and true, patient, and ordering all things in mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if we sin, we are thine, knowing thy greatness: and if we sin not, we know that we are counted with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For to know thee is perfect justice: and to know thy justice, and thy power, is the root of immortality.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the invention of mischievous men hath not deceived us, nor the shadow of a picture, a fruitless labour, a graven figure with divers colours,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sight whereof enticeth the fool to lust after it, and he loveth the lifeless figure of a dead image.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The lovers of evil things deserve to have no better things to trust in, both they that make them, and they that love them, and they that worship them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The potter also tempering soft earth, with labour fashioneth every vessel for our service, and of the same clay he maketh both vessels that are for clean uses, and likewise such as serve to the contrary: but what is the use of these vessels, the potter is the judge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And of the same clay by a vain labour he maketh a god: he who a little before was made of earth himself, and a little after returneth to the same out of which he was taken, when his life, which was lent him, shall be called for again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But his care is, not that he shall labour, nor that his life is short, but he striveth with the goldsmiths and silversmiths: and he endeavoureth to do like the workers in brass, and counteth it a glory to make vain things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For his heart is ashes, and his hope vain earth and his life more base than clay:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forasmuch as he knew not his maker, and him that inspired into him the soul that worketh, and that breathed into him a living spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea, and they have counted our life a pastime and the business of life to be gain, and that we must be getting every way, even out of evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For that man knoweth that he offendeth above all others, who of earthly matter maketh brittle vessels, and graven gods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But all the enemies of thy people that hold them in subjection, are foolish, and unhappy, and proud beyond measure:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they have esteemed all the idols of the heathens for gods, which neither have the use of eyes to see, nor noses to draw breath, nor ears to hear, nor fingers of hands to handle, and as for their feet, they are slow to walk.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For man made them: and he that borroweth his own breath, fashioned them. For no man can make a god like to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For being mortal himself, he formeth a dead thing with his wicked hands. For he is better than they whom he worshippeth, because he indeed hath lived, though he were mortal, but they never.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, they worship also the vilest creatures: but things without sense, compared to these, are worse than they.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea, neither by sight can any man see good of these beasts. But they have fled from the praise of God, and from his blessing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For these things, and by the like things to these, they were worthily punished, and were destroyed by a multitude of beasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Instead of which punishment, dealing well with thy people, thou gavest them their desire of delicious food, of a new taste, preparing for them quails for their meat:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To the end, that they indeed desiring food, by means of those things that were shewn and sent among them, might loath even that which was necessary to satisfy their desire. But these, after suffering want for a short time, tasted a new meat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it was requisite that inevitable destruction should come upon them that exercised tyranny: but to these it should only be shewn how their enemies were destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when the fierce rage of beasts came upon these, they were destroyed by the bitings of crooked serpents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thy wrath endured not for ever, but they were troubled for a short time for their correction, having a sign of salvation, to put them in remembrance of the commandment of thy law.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he that turned to it, was not healed by that which he saw, but by thee, the Saviour of all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in this thou didst shew to our enemies, that thou art he who deliverest from all evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the bitings of locusts, and of flies, killed them, and there was found no remedy for their life: because they were worthy to be destroyed by such things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But not even the teeth of venomous serpents overcame thy children: for thy mercy came and healed them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they were examined for the remembrance of thy words, and were quickly healed, lest falling into deep forgetfulness, they might not be able to use thy help.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it was neither herb, nor mollifying plaster, that healed them, but thy word, O Lord, which healeth all things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is thou, O Lord, that hast power of life and death, and leadest down to the gates of death, and bringest back again:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man indeed killeth through malice, and when the spirit is gone forth, it shall not return, neither shall he call back the soul that is received:
