source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But it is impossible to escape thy hand:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the wicked that denied to know thee, were scourged by the strength of thy arm, being persecuted by strange waters, and hail, and rain, and consumed by fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And which was wonderful, in water, which extinguisheth all things, the fire had more force: for the world fighteth for the just.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For at one time the fire was mitigated, that the beasts which were sent against the wicked might not be burnt, but that they might see, and perceive that they were persecuted by the judgment of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And at another time the fire, above its own power, burnt in the midst of water, to destroy the fruits of a wicked land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Instead of which things, thou didst feed thy people with the food of angels, and gavest them bread from heaven, prepared without labour; having in it all that is delicious, and the sweetness of every taste.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy sustenance shewed thy sweetness to thy children, and serving every man’s will, it was turned to what every man liked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But snow and ice endured the force of fire, and melted not: that they might know that the fire, burning in the hail, and flashing in the rain, destroyed the fruits of the enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But this same again, that the just might be nourished, did even forget its own strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the creature serving thee, the Creator, is made fierce against the unjust for their punishment: and abateth its strength for the benefit of them that trust in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore even then it was transformed into all things, and was obedient to thy grace, that nourisheth all, according to the will of them that desired it of thee:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That thy children, O Lord, whom thou lovedst, might know that it is not the growing of fruits that nourisheth men, but thy word preserveth them that believe in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For that which could not be destroyed by fire, being warmed with a little sunbeam, presently melted away:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That it might be known to all, that we ought to prevent the sun to bless thee, and adore thee at the dawning of the light.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the hope of the unthankful shall melt away as the winter’s ice, and shall run off as unprofitable water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy judgments, O Lord, are great, and thy words cannot be expressed: therefore undisciplined souls have erred.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For while the wicked thought to be able to have dominion over the holy nation, they themselves being fettered with the bonds of darkness, and a long night, shut up in their houses, lay there exiled from the eternal providence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And while they thought to lie hid in their obscure sins, they were scattered under a dark veil of forgetfullness, being horribly afraid, and troubled with exceeding great astonishment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For neither did the den that held them, keep them from fear: for noises coming down troubled them, and sad visions appearing to them, affrighted them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And no power of fire could give them light, neither could the bright flames of the stars enlighten that horrible night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But there appeared to them a sudden fire, very dreadful: and being struck with the fear of that face, which was not seen, they thought the things which they saw to be worse:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the delusions of their magic art were put down, and their boasting of wisdom was reproachfully rebuked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they who promised to drive away fears and troubles from a sick soul, were sick themselves of a fear worthy to be laughed at.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For though no terrible thing disturbed them: yet being scared with the passing by of beasts, and hissing of serpents, they died for fear and denying that they saw the air, which could by no means be avoided.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whereas wickedness is fearful, it beareth witness of its condemnation: for a troubled conscience always forecasteth grievous things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For fear is nothing else but a yielding up of the succours from thought.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And while there is less expectation from within, the greater doth it count the ignorance of that cause which bringeth the torment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that during that night, in which nothing could be done, and which came upon them from the lowest and deepest hell, slept the same sleep,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Were sometimes molested with the fear of monsters, sometimes fainted away, their soul failing them: for a sudden and unlooked for fear was come upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, if any of them had fallen down, he was kept shut up in prison without irons.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For if any one were a husbandman, or a shepherd, or a labourer in the field, and was suddenly overtaken, he endured a necessity from which he could not fly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they were all bound together with one chain of darkness. Whether it were a whistling wind, or the melodious voice of birds, among the spreading branches of trees, or a fall of water running down with violence,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Or the mighty noise of stones tumbling down, or the running that could not be seen of beasts playing together, or the roaring voice of wild beasts, or a rebounding echo from the highest mountains: these things made them to swoon for fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the whole world was enlightened, with a clear light, and none were hindered in their labours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But over them only was spread a heavy night, an image of that darkness which was to come upon them. But they were to themselves more grievous than the darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thy saints had a very great light, and they heard their voice indeed, but did not see their shape. And because they also did not suffer the same things, they glorified thee:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that before had been wronged, gave thanks, because they were not hurt now: and asked this gift, that there might be a difference.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore they received a burning pillar of fire for a guide of the way which they knew not, and thou gavest them a harmless sun of a good entertainment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The others indeed were worthy to be deprived of light, and imprisoned in darkness, who kept thy children shut up, by whom the pure light of the law was to be given to the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas they thought to kill the babes of the just: one child being cast forth, and saved to reprove them, thou tookest away a multitude of their children, and destroyedst them altogether in a mighty water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For that night was known before by our fathers, that assuredly knowing what oaths they had trusted to, they might be of better courage.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So thy people received the salvation of the just, and destruction of the unjust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as thou didst punish the adversaries so thou didst also encourage and glorify us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the just children of good men were offering sacrifice secretly, and they unanimously ordered a law of justice: that the just should receive both good and evil alike, singing now the praises of the fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But on the other side there sounded an ill according cry of the enemies, and a lamentable mourning was heard for the children that were bewailed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the servant suffered the same punishment as the master, and a common man suffered in like manner as the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So all alike had innumerable dead, with one kind of death. Neither were the living sufficient to bury them: for in one moment the noblest offspring of them was destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whereas they would not believe any thing before by reason of the enchantments, then first upon the destruction of the firstborn, they acknowledged the people to be of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For while all things were in quiet silence, and the night was in the midst of her course,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy Almighty word leaped down from heaven from thy royal throne, as a fierce conqueror into the midst of the land of destruction,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With a sharp sword carrying thy unfeigned commandment, and he stood and filled all things with death, and standing on the earth, reached even to heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then suddenly visions of evil dreams troubled them, and fears unlooked for came upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And one thrown here, another there, half dead, shewed the cause of his death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the visions that troubled them foreshewed these things, lest they should perish, and not know why they suffered these evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the just also were afterwards touched by an assault of death, and there was a disturbance of the multitude in the wilderness: but thy wrath did not long continue;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a blameless man made haste to pry for the people, bringing forth the shield of his ministry, prayer, and by incense making supplication, withstood the wrath, and put an end to the calamity, shewing that he was thy servant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he overcame the disturbance, not by strength of body nor with force of arms, but with a word he subdued him that punished them, alleging the oath and covenant made with the fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when they were now fallen down dead by heaps one upon another, he stood between and stayed the assault, and cut off the way to the living.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in the priestly robe which he wore, was the whole world: and in the four rows of the stones, the glory of the fathers was graven, and thy majesty was written upon the diadem of his head.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to these the destroyer gave place, and was afraid of them: for the proof only of wrath was enough.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as to the wicked, even to the end there came upon them wrath without mercy. For he knew before also what they would do:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when they had given them leave to depart and had sent them away with great care, they repented and pursued after them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For whilst they were yet mourning, and lamenting at the graves of the dead, they took up another foolish device: and pursued them as fugitives whom they had pressed to be gone:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a necessity, of which they were worthy, brought them to this end: and they lost the remembrance of those things which had happened, that their punishment might fill up what was wanting to their torments:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that thy people might wonderfully pass through, but they might find a new death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For every creature, according to its kind was fashioned again as from the beginning, obeying thy commandments, that thy children might be kept without hurt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For a cloud overshadowed their camps and where water was before, dry land appeared, and in the Red Sea a way without hindrance, and out of the great deep a springing field:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Through which all the nation passed which was protected with thy hand, seeing thy miracles and wonders.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they fed on their food like horses, and they skipped like lambs, praising thee, O Lord, who hadst delivered them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they were yet mindful of those things which had been done in the time of their sojourning, how the ground brought forth flies instead of cattle, and how the river cast up a multitude of frogs instead of fishes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And at length they saw a new generation of birds, when being led by their appetite, they asked for delicate meats.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For to satisfy their desire, the quail came up to them from the sea: and punishments came upon the sinners, not without foregoing signs by the force of thunders: for they suffered justly according to their own wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they exercised a more detestable inhospitality than any: others indeed received not strangers unknown to them, but these brought their guests into bondage that had deserved well of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And not only so, but in another respect also they were worse: for the others against their will received the strangers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But these grievously afflicted them whom they had received with joy, and who lived under the same laws.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they were struck with blindness: as those others were at the doors of the just man, when they were covered with sudden darkness, and every one sought the passage of his own door.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For while the elements are changed in themselves, as in an instrument the sound of the quality is changed, yet all keep their sound: which may clearly be perceived by the very sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the things of the land were turned into things of the water: and the things that before swam in the water passed upon the land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fire had power in water above its own virtue, and the water forgot its quenching nature.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: On the other side, the flames wasted not the flesh of corruptible animals walking therein, neither did they melt that good food, which was apt to melt as ice. For in all things thou didst magnify thy people, O Lord, and didst honour them, and didst not despise them, but didst assist them at all times, and in every place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
